name: East Erickson mining district
short_description: Historic mining district on BLM land near Tooele offering free public rockhounding for gold, copper, and quartz specimens. Perfect for prospectors seeking productive claims history.
description:
The East Erickson mining district in Tooele County sits within Utah's productive metallic mineral belt, where late Cretaceous to Tertiary mineralization created veins and disseminated deposits of gold, copper, and silver. This BLM-managed area preserves both active and historical mining activity, making it an excellent destination for rockhounds interested in understanding mining geology firsthand.

The district benefits from its location within the Great Basin's horst-and-graben terrain, where Basin and Range extension exposed mineralized rock that hosted significant mining operations throughout the 20th century. Today, the exposed geology makes it straightforward to spot mineralized outcrops, quartz veins, and colorful copper-stained rocks that indicate metal-bearing zones.

Expect to find scattered specimens of chalcedony and quartz along washes and hillsides, along with oxidized copper minerals and occasionally small flakes or dust of native gold in quartz veins. The terrain is moderate and well-suited to explorers with basic prospecting experience. While the land is open to public collecting under standard BLM recreational use rules, the mining history means old shafts and workings may be present - stay aware of potential hazards.

This location rewards patience and geological knowledge. Bring a loupe, hammer, and willingness to examine old mine dumps and exposed rock faces for the quartz veins that historically produced metal.
history: The East Erickson district was developed as a copper and gold prospect during the late 1800s and early 1900s, when prospectors followed quartz veins and chalcocite deposits exposed by Basin and Range faulting. Tertiary porphyry systems in the Oquirrh Range and adjacent valleys created the metal-bearing host rocks that miners targeted, leaving behind a legacy of small claims and working shafts that reveal the subsurface geology.
tips: Search exposed ridge faces and small canyons for white quartz veins - these are your best indicator of mineralization and often carry visible copper staining or rare gold flakes. Examine old mine dumps carefully, as they contain broken rock from productive zones, though handle loose material cautiously. Bring a magnet to test for magnetite and a loupe to spot chalcopyrite (brassy copper mineral) and gold dust in quartz. Early morning light makes copper-stained outcrops easier to spot on hillsides.
terrain_notes: Elevation ranges from 5,000 to 6,500 feet across rolling hills and shallow washes typical of the Basin and Range. Terrain is moderately steep in places with scattered sage, pinyon, and juniper. Rocky footing is the norm - wear sturdy boots. Most productive areas are within 1-3 miles of road access via rough dirt roads suitable for high-clearance vehicles. No water available on-site.
best_season: April - June and September - October. Spring and fall offer mild temperatures and stable weather. Summer heat can reach 85-90 degrees F and winter snowfall may restrict road access, though the area remains accessible most of the year.
written_directions: From Tooele, take UT 199 (Tooele Valley Boulevard) east toward Ophir. Continue on local Forest Service and BLM roads eastward into the foothills. GPS coordinates 39.910969, -112.511248 mark the approximate district center. A high-clearance vehicle is recommended. Check current BLM office conditions before traveling, as minor roads may be rough or seasonally closed.
road_conditions: Rough BLM roads, high-clearance recommended. Some washes may be impassable after rain. No maintained pavement beyond the town approach.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along BLM roads near active collecting areas. No dedicated lot. Space is available but limited.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No blasting. Respect active mining claims if posted. Stay clear of open shafts and unstable mining workings.
rules:

nearest_services: Tooele, approximately 15 miles west. Full services including gas, food, water, and supplies available in town.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven rocky footing and steep hillsides in places. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots required. Road access may not accommodate low-clearance vehicles. Children should be able to walk 1-3 miles on rough terrain with adult supervision.

faq.question: What can you find rockhounding at East Erickson mining district in Utah?
faq.answer: You can find native gold, copper minerals, silver, quartz veins, and chalcedony specimens. The district is a historic mining area where oxidized copper deposits and gold-bearing quartz veins are exposed on hillsides and in washes. Small flakes of gold dust in quartz and colorful copper-stained rocks are common finds for patient rockhounders.

faq.question: Is East Erickson mining district free to collect rockhounding specimens?
faq.answer: Yes. East Erickson is open BLM land with no entry fee or permit required for recreational rockhounding. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day of specimens for personal use at no cost. This is one of the best free rockhounding sites near Tooele.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Tooele at East Erickson?
faq.answer: April through June and September through October offer the best conditions. Spring and fall have mild temperatures around 60-70 degrees F. Summer heat can exceed 85 degrees F, and winter snow may affect road access, though the area is accessible most of the year.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at East Erickson mining district?
faq.answer: Bring a rock hammer, a 10x loupe for examining specimens, a magnet to test for magnetite, and a sturdy collecting bag. Wear heavy boots for rocky terrain. A GPS unit helps navigate the rough BLM roads. Bring plenty of water - there is none on-site. A field guide to Utah minerals is helpful for identifying copper minerals and quartz veins.

faq.question: Is East Erickson mining district a good rockhounding spot for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, it is beginner-friendly. The exposed geology makes it easy to spot mineralized outcrops, and quartz veins with copper staining are visual guides to productive collecting areas. The moderate terrain is suitable for families with children ages 8 and up, though the rough access road requires a high-clearance vehicle.

faq.question: Are there any hazards or restrictions I should know about rockhounding at East Erickson?
faq.answer: Avoid open mine shafts and unstable mining workings - these are hazardous and unstable. Watch for sharp material and loose rock on hillsides. The area is remote with spotty cell service, so bring communication tools and tell someone your itinerary. Summer heat requires extra water. Respect any posted mining claims.
